destination.vibe: A mid-sized Southern city with a world-class free art museum, one of the longest intact stretches of Victorian row houses in the US, a brewery-and-restaurant scene anchored in Scott's Addition and the Fan, and a James River cutting straight through downtown with swimming holes and herons accessible entirely on foot.

flights.outbound_dates: Late July 2026 (e.g. Jul 29) — mid-week departures cheapest
flights.notes: Spirit operates nonstop DTW→RIC. Ultra-low base fare but add bags and seat selection. Book 5–8 weeks out. Cheapest days: Tuesday/Wednesday departures. Best for carry-on-only travel.

flights.outbound_dates: Late July 2026 (flexible Jul 28–Aug 2 window) — midweek lowest
flights.notes: Delta runs frequent nonstops DTW→RIC. Nonstop roundtrip from $350–$450 in peak summer. Includes carry-on bag. Book on Sundays for ~6% savings. Best choice for flexibility and reliability.

flights.outbound_dates: Flexible late July / early August 2026 — Tuesday/Wednesday lowest
flights.notes: American serves DTW→RIC via connection at Charlotte (CLT) or Philadelphia (PHL). Roundtrip from ~$280–$350. Longer travel day but sometimes significantly cheaper than Delta nonstop — check aa.com fare calendar.

lodging.name: Fan District Victorian Row House (Entire Home — Airbnb)
lodging.highlights: Entire Victorian row house on or near Strawberry Street in Richmond's most walkable historic neighborhood — steps from restaurants, coffee shops, the VMFA, and Carytown, with the neighborhood feel that no hotel can replicate.

lodging.name: Graduate by Hilton Richmond
lodging.neighborhood: Downtown / Fan District edge (301 W Franklin St, near VCU)
lodging.highlights: University-district boutique hotel with rooftop terrace overlooking the VCU Institute for Contemporary Art — walkable to VMFA, the Fan, and downtown; consistent mid-range quality with a good location for everything.

lodging.name: Quirk Hotel Richmond
lodging.highlights: Boutique gallery-hotel with rotating art installations, rooftop bar, and on-site restaurant Maple & Pine — the most design-forward hotel in Richmond, a Destination by Hyatt property in the heart of the arts district.

lodging.name: The Jefferson Hotel
lodging.neighborhood: Downtown Richmond (Franklin St at Adams St)
lodging.highlights: National Historic Landmark opened in 1895 — the staircase reportedly inspired Gone With the Wind, Lemaire restaurant is Richmond's finest dining, and the grandeur is genuine rather than corporate.

itinerary.overview: Richmond is a mid-sized Southern city that punches well above its weight: a world-class free art museum (VMFA), one of the longest intact stretches of Victorian architecture in the US, a brewery-and-restaurant scene anchored in Scott's Addition and the Fan, and a James River cutting straight through downtown with swimming holes, herons, and Class IV rapids accessible entirely on foot. Ten days lets you sink into neighborhoods rather than race through them — the Fan on a Tuesday morning with coffee and a bakery, Church Hill at dusk, Carytown on a Saturday, Shockoe Bottom for history and oysters.

days.title: Arrival + The Fan Orientation
days.morning: Fly in from DTW, land at RIC. Rideshare or rent a car (Uber/Lyft are plentiful from RIC; ~20 minutes to the Fan). Check into your Fan District Airbnb or Graduate Hotel. Walk the length of Monument Avenue from Belvidere Street west: observe the grand mansions, the empty pedestals where Confederate statues stood (only Arthur Ashe's statue remains at the Boulevard intersection), and the remarkable tree canopy. This sets the tone for Richmond's complicated, evolving relationship with its past.
days.afternoon: Walk Strawberry Street — the Fan's informal village center. Stop at Garnett's Cafe (2001 Park Ave) for a late lunch: the Scuffletown Chicken Salad Sandwich and one of their celebrated pies. Afterward, head to the VMFA (200 N Arthur Ashe Blvd) — free admission, no reservation needed — and spend 2 hours with the permanent collection. Don't miss Kehinde Wiley's 'Rumors of War' out front, the Faberge collection (largest outside Russia), and the Art Nouveau galleries.
days.evening: Dinner at Beaucoup (111 N Robinson St) in the Fan — Richmond's best seafood and oyster spot, French-ish bistro format. Order the oysters and whatever fish they have. Then walk to The Jasper (3101 W Cary St) in Carytown — named by many as one of the best cocktail bars on the East Coast. Two drinks and settle in.

days.title: VMFA Deep Dive + Carytown
days.morning: Return to the VMFA at 10am when it opens. Spend the full morning in the wings you didn't reach yesterday: the American galleries, the African art collection, the modern and contemporary floors. The VMFA is genuinely on par with major metropolitan museums and free admission means you can linger without guilt. Check icavcu.org for any current special exhibitions.
days.afternoon: Walk or rideshare to Carytown (W Cary St between Thompson and Boulevard). This nine-block stretch of independent shops is the city's most enjoyable afternoon: browse For the Love of Chocolate, World of Mirth (bizarre toys and gifts), and any bookshop you see. Lunch at Mom's Siam (Thai, 3622 W Cary St) — the green curry is legendary. Stop at Gelati Celesti for ice cream; try the 'Just Ask' flavor.
days.evening: Check the Byrd Theatre schedule (2908 W Cary St) — Richmond's 1928 movie palace showing films nightly for $4–8. On Friday and Saturday nights the Mighty Wurlitzer organ plays before the film. If nothing appeals, walk to Can Can Brasserie (3120 W Cary St) for dinner — French bistro with a gorgeous Belle Époque interior.

days.title: Jackson Ward History + Soul Food
days.morning: Start at the Black History Museum & Cultural Center of Virginia (122 W Leigh St) in Jackson Ward, housed in the historic Leigh Street Armory. This neighborhood was the 'Harlem of the South' and 'Black Wall Street of America' — home to Maggie L. Walker (first woman bank president in the US). Visit the Maggie L. Walker National Historic Site (600 N 2nd St), a free NPS site with ranger-led tours of her home offered daily.
days.afternoon: Lunch at Mama J's (415 N 1st St) — fried chicken, collard greens, mac and cheese. Cash and card, no reservations, worth any wait. After lunch, walk the mural corridors of Jackson Ward — it rivals Scott's Addition for large-scale street art. The intersection of Broad and Belvidere is a good starting point. Visit the Bill 'Bojangles' Robinson statue at Adams and Leigh.
days.evening: Dinner at Restaurant Adarra (1 W Marshall St) — Basque pintxos done with Virginia ingredients, outstanding wine list. Small plates format; order 6–8 dishes. Walk over to Soul Taco (2005 W Broad St) afterward for late-night tacos: the root beer braised oxtail taco is exceptional.

days.title: Church Hill + The James River
days.morning: Start at Sub Rosa Bakery (620 N 25th St) in Church Hill — labor-intensive sourdough and pastries using Virginia grain, one of the best bakeries in the South. Eat your pastry while walking up the hill to St. John's Church (2401 E Broad St), where Patrick Henry gave his 'Give Me Liberty or Give Me Death' speech in 1775. The hilltop views over the James River and Shockoe Valley are exceptional.
days.afternoon: Walk down to Belle Isle via the pedestrian suspension bridge off Tredegar St. Belle Isle is a 54-acre island in the middle of the James with flat rocks, Civil War-era ruins, and river access. Bring water, sunscreen, and shoes you don't mind getting wet — wade in the rocky pools, sun on the granite slabs. This is where Richmond locals actually spend their summer afternoons. Then walk Tredegar Street along the north bank past the American Civil War Museum (500 Tredegar St) — the building exterior and grounds are free even if you skip the $15 interior ticket.
days.evening: Dinner in Shockoe Slip: Sam Miller's (1210 E Cary St) for fresh seafood and raw bar — this is the restaurant that sparked the Shockoe revival in 1972 and it's still excellent. Wander the cobblestone streets of the Slip afterward, some of Richmond's oldest surviving commercial architecture.

days.title: Scott's Addition Brewery District
days.morning: Sleep in. Scott's Addition doesn't get going until afternoon. Walk to Blanchard's Coffee for the morning. Then head to Brambly Park (2901 W Leigh St) — a 2-acre outdoor urban winery and cider taproom with lawn seating, often has weekend morning events.
days.afternoon: The Scott's Addition brewery crawl. Start at The Veil Brewing Co. (2545 W Leigh St) — widely regarded as one of the best IPA and hazy ale breweries on the East Coast. Then Ardent Craft Ales (3200 W Leigh St), then Strangeways Brewing (2277 Dabney Rd). Stop at ZZQ Texas Craft Barbeque (3201 W Moore St, open Wed–Sun until sold out) — Texas-style brisket that won Food & Wine's best in Virginia. Also check Blue Bee Cider (1320 Dell Ave) for Virginia heirloom apple ciders.
days.evening: Dinner at Lost Letter on the edge of Scott's Addition for beef tartare and butterkin squash gnocchi. Walk back along W Broad Street and catch the live music drifting out of various bar patios on a warm summer evening.

days.title: Maymont Estate + Oregon Hill + L'Opossum
days.morning: Head to Maymont (1700 Hampton St) at 10am — free admission. This 100-acre Victorian estate donated to the city in 1926 is a genuine gem: Japanese Garden, Italian Garden, Arboretum. Then visit the Virginia Wildlife Trail — 40 acres of rescued native animals including black bears, bobcats, bald eagles, and Great Blue Herons.
days.afternoon: Walk or rideshare to Oregon Hill — one of Richmond's most bohemian, working-class historic neighborhoods clinging to a hillside above the James. Find the overlook at South Pine Street for the best elevated river views in the city. Then head to Pony Pasture Rapids (7200 Riverside Dr, south bank) — Richmond's most popular swimming spot on the James, with natural pools, rock slides, and sunbathers. Bring a towel.
days.evening: Dinner at L'Opossum (626 China St, Oregon Hill) — arguably Richmond's most singular restaurant: French technique applied to eclectic Virginia-Southern mashups (escargot with ham biscuits). The interior is maximalist and theatrical. Reservations essential — James Beard Award-recognized chef David Shannon, Best Chef Mid-Atlantic.

days.title: ICA VCU + Shockoe Bottom History
days.morning: Visit the Institute for Contemporary Art at VCU (601 W Broad St) — always free, Tuesday–Sunday 10am–5pm. The Steven Holl-designed building is worth seeing even before you enter (the glass facade shifts with daylight). Check icavcu.org for current exhibitions. Spend 90 minutes here.
days.afternoon: Rideshare to Shockoe Bottom. Visit the Virginia Holocaust Museum (2000 E Cary St, $10) — one of the most moving regional Holocaust museums in the country, inside a beautifully renovated warehouse. Then walk to 17th Street Farmers Market (100 N 17th St) — one of the oldest continuously operating farmers markets in the US, now a weekend outdoor market. Visit the Reconciliation Triangle nearby, which acknowledges Richmond's role as the largest domestic slave trading hub in pre-Civil War America.
days.evening: The Poe Museum (1914 E Main St) runs 'Unhappy Hour' cocktail events in the Enchanted Garden on select evenings — check poemuseum.org for dates. If not scheduled, visit during afternoon hours (Tue–Sun, $12). Dinner at Addis Ethiopian (2306 Jefferson Ave, Shockoe Bottom) for housemade injera and outstanding vegetarian stews.

days.title: Day Trip: Route 5 Historic Byway + Upper Shirley Vineyards
days.morning: Rent a car for the day or rideshare east. Drive ~30 minutes on Route 5 (the historic scenic byway) through Charles City County. Upper Shirley Vineyards (600 Shirley Plantation Rd) is a working vineyard with 22 acres producing Viognier and sparkling rosé. Tastings from 11am. The drive along Route 5 passes Berkeley Plantation (birthplace of William Henry Harrison, site of the first Thanksgiving according to some historians).
days.afternoon: Stop at Shirley Plantation (501 Shirley Plantation Rd) — the oldest active plantation in the US (1613), still owned by the Hill-Carter family, with outstanding original outbuildings. Return to Richmond via the River Road scenic route. Late afternoon: seat at Hardywood Park Craft Brewery (2410 Ownby Ln) for outdoor beer garden time.
days.evening: Dinner at Edo's Squid (411 N Harrison St, Downtown) — loud Italian in a cramped room with garlic-forward pasta and the best calamari in Richmond. No reservations; arrive by 5:30pm or expect a wait. Check The National (708 E Broad St) for what's playing — one of the best mid-size music rooms on the East Coast.

days.title: Fan Neighborhood Walk + Museum District + Celladora
days.morning: A slow Fan morning: walk the grid of streets between Park Avenue and Floyd Avenue, from Belvidere to Boulevard. The Fan contains one of the longest intact stretches of Victorian architecture in the US — Queen Anne, Italianate, Colonial Revival row houses. Stop into Susie's Delicatessen for a late morning bagel or matzo ball soup. Then browse the Virginia Museum of History and Culture (428 N Arthur Ashe Blvd, next to VMFA) — excellent permanent exhibition on Virginia's full arc from Indigenous peoples to today.
days.afternoon: Visit the Valentine Museum (1015 E Clay St, downtown) — Richmond's city history museum with guided walking tours of neighborhoods. Check if they have a scheduled walking tour of the Fan or Church Hill. Afternoon coffee at North End Juice Co. for fresh smoothies at a walk-up window. Then an hour back at VMFA — the cafe inside (Best Cafe in RVA, multiple years) is excellent for a late lunch.
days.evening: Splurge dinner at Celladora (Fan District) — described by The Infatuation as the best restaurant in Richmond, technically a wine shop but the French-leaning small plates are exceptional, natural wine focus. Reservations essential. Or if budget is tight: Stella's Greek (1012 Lafayette St) for moussaka and braised lamb at excellent value — a Richmond institution.

days.title: Last Morning on the James + Farewell
days.morning: One final James River morning: walk Belle Isle at sunrise, or rideshare to Pony Pasture for a last swim if it's warm. Pick up pastries from Sub Rosa Bakery or Shyndigz (the banana pudding cake and layer cakes went viral for a reason). Walk Monument Avenue one last time in the morning cool before the heat sets in.
days.afternoon: Final lunch at Joe's Inn (205 N Shields Ave, The Fan) — 73-year Richmond institution, always packed, beloved for spaghetti, burgers, and consistency. Walk Strawberry Street one last time. Pack up and rideshare to RIC (Richmond International Airport, about 20–25 minutes from the Fan). Flight home to DTW.

tips: The Fan is the best base: most restaurants, coffee shops, and bars are within a 15-minute walk of each other, and you can easily walk to the VMFA, Carytown, and Oregon Hill. The Airbnb Victorian row houses give you a neighborhood feel that no hotel can match.

The James River is Richmond's secret amenity. Pony Pasture (south side, accessible by car/rideshare) is safer and more popular for swimming than Belle Isle — the rocky pools there are genuinely beautiful and locals treat them as their backyard. Don't miss it on a hot day.

Richmond's restaurant scene is best Tuesday through Thursday — many top spots are closed Monday, and Friday/Saturday brings longer waits. L'Opossum, Celladora, and Beaucoup all require advance reservations; book them before you leave Detroit.

The VMFA is completely free for the permanent collection — there's no reason not to go multiple times. 90 minutes twice beats one exhausting four-hour marathon visit. The cafe inside (Best Cafe in RVA award, multiple years) is also excellent for lunch.
Scott's Addition has almost no parking on weekends — Uber there and walk between breweries. The Veil has lines on weekend afternoons; arrive at opening (noon Saturday) to avoid the worst waits.
ZZQ sells out by early afternoon most days (open Wed–Sun, 11am). Get there by 11:30am or you'll miss the brisket. They are genuinely sold out by 2pm on busy days — this is not an exaggeration.
